text:
Hylocereus polyrhizus and Hylocereus undatus, pitaya or dragon fruit, have become one of the most marketable fruits all over the world. Although this fruit has gained a great deal of popularity, there has been very few studies on the chemical composition and nutritional value of the peel of this fruit, which are simply thrown away as byproducts during juice production. In this study, the extraction of bioactive compounds from the peels of the two species was done using supercritical carbon dioxide extraction and identified using gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC-MS).

Through comparison of the chromatographic profiles of the extracts obtained from H. polyrhizus and H. undatus, we identified β-amyrin, α-amyrin, octacosane, and γ-sitosterol as the main components, while β-amyrin, γ-sitosterol, and octadecane were the predominant constituents in the extract of H. undatus. Analysis of their bioactivities indicated that both extracts displayed obvious cytotoxicity on the PC3, Bcap-37, and MGC-803 cell lines, with IC50 ranging from 0.61 to 0.73 mg/mL, while further test on the individual main components confirmed the contributions of the main components to their cytotoxicity as well as their antioxidant abilities determined using radical scavenging assays. Our results indicated that pitaya peel that has been regarded as an agricultural by-product is still rich in bioactive compounds and holds great potential for further development into nutritional food resources and also as the medicinal resources.
